title: Libprocess links will not generate an ExitedEvent if the socket creation fails
description: Noticed this while inspecting nearby code for potential races.  Normally, when a libprocess actor (the """"linkee"""") links to a remote process, it does the following: 1) Create a socket. 2) Connect to the remote process (asynchronous). 3) Check the connection succeeded.  If (2) or (3) fail, the linkee will receive a {{ExitedEvent}}, which indicates that the link broke.  In case (1) fails, there is no {{ExitedEvent}}: https://github.com/apache/mesos/blob/7c833abbec9c9e4eb51d67f7a8e7a8d0870825f8/3rdparty/libprocess/src/process.cpp#L1558-L1562
reasoning: The ticket involves fixing a specific issue in libprocess where ExitedEvent is not generated when socket creation fails. The scope is relatively contained, but the integration surface with the remote process and the asynchronous connection may add complexity. The provided code reference helps to narrow down the affected area.

title: iOS: Differences between new permission request responses
description:
While working in the [5.1 sample|https://github.com/<USER>developer-relations/appc-sample-ti510] I noticed some differences between the responses of the new permission request methods. I created a separate test case to show the differences so we can address them in a next release. Nothing big, except for the first bullet maybe.    * Unlike the others, the callback for {{requestLocationPermissions()}} is only called the first time. You have to force close and then re-open the app for it to work again.  * The response for {{requestLocationPermissions}} also has {{authorizationStatus}}, which would be very useful to have in the response for the others as well.  * On success, the response for {{requestLocationPermissions}} has an empty string for {{error}} where it should either have a message or be undefined.  * When unknown, {{requestContactsPermissions}} has a not very helpful _Access Denied_ {{error}} instead of just being undefined like {{Calendar}} and {{Camera}}.  * Unless permission was granted, the {{error}} for {{requestLocationPermissions}} is always _The user is unable to allow access to location._, which is not very helpful or even misleading since the user is perfectly able, he just denied permission to the app. Calendar and Contacts have better error messages.  * When denied, the response for {{requestCameraPermissions}} has no {{error}} like the others.  * I have no idea where {{code}} comes from but if I deny it (probably by coincidence) correctly maps to {{AUTHORIZATION_DENIED}} for Calendar and Contacts. Would be helpful to have the meaning of this code documented.  * In iOS 8, denying contacts permission sends success response to callback.     h2. Sample    Don't forget to add {{<key>NSLocationAlwaysUsageDescription</key><string>Deny or grant permission to test different scenarios.</string>}} to {{tiapp.xml}}.    {code:javascript}  var win = Ti.UI.createWindow({   backgroundColor: 'white'  });    var btn = Ti.UI.createButton({   title: 'Request Permissions'  });    btn.addEventListener('click', function(e) {     console.log('

title: Remove database and collection cleanup from $config.teardown functions
description: As mentioned in [this comment|https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-34548?focusedCommentId=1868113&page=com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.issuetabpanels:comment-tabpanel#comment-1868113] of SERVER-34548, having FSM workloads drop their (unique) database or collection in the {{$config.teardown()}} function undermines our data consistency checks as the contents will be deleted before the resmoke.py hooks run. title: iOS9: Adopt IPv6 requirement for App Store Submissions
description: “Because IPv6 support is so critical to ensuring your applications work across the world for every customer, we are making it an AppStore submission requirement, starting with iOS 9.”    http://www.internetsociety.org/deploy360/blog/2015/06/apple-will-require-ipv6-support-for-all-ios-9-apps/    h4. Quote from link  {quote}  Three steps all developers can take to make sure their applications work over IPv6 networks:  - Use the networking frameworks (for example, “NSURLSession”)  - Avoid use of IPv4-specific APIs  - Avoid hard-coded IP addresses    Essentially, if app developers are using the higher level APIs and frameworks and aren’t hacking around at the IP layer, their apps should probably “just work” on top of either IPv4 or IPv6.  This is an important point – most iOS developers probably do not need to do anything on the development side. Assuming they have followed best practices in coding and are using the iOS networking frameworks, they should be all set.  Some developers, though, may be using lower level APIs that may involve direct usage of IPv4 addresses. Some developers may also be using the user’s IPv4 address as an identifier or for logging or configuration purposes.  But again, most iOS developers probably don’t need to change their code to support IPv6.  {quote}    Apple Guide: [here|https://developer.apple.com/library/prerelease/ios/documentation/NetworkingInternetWeb/Conceptual/NetworkingOverview/UnderstandingandPreparingfortheIPv6Transition/UnderstandingandPreparingfortheIPv6Transition.html#//apple_ref/doc/uid/TP40010220-CH213-SW1]
reasoning: The ticket requires adopting IPv6 support for App Store submissions, which involves reviewing and potentially modifying the Titanium SDK's networking frameworks. The scope is relatively well-defined, but there may be unknowns related to the implementation details and potential edge cases. The integration surface is also a consideration, as the change needs to work seamlessly with existing iOS 9 functionality.

title: Mix-in Alloy Github edit URLs
description: For TIDOC-2058, I had thought we could just add the @editurl tags to the Alloy docs manually (https://github.com/appcelerator/alloy/pull/659). This works, but preferably this feature would have no dependencies on the Alloy code repos, and be handled as a doc build task.    This PR adds a folder with a JS file that provides the edit URLs for each public Alloy class. JSDuck auto-magically merges this with the core Alloy docs. This folder is added to the {{$alloyDirs}} script variable. I submitted a PR to revert the merged changes to the Alloy repo.
reasoning: The ticket involves adding a new feature to the Titanium Mobile Platform by creating a JS file to provide edit URLs for Alloy classes. The scope is relatively contained, with a clear goal of integrating with JSDuck. However, the complexity of the JSDuck integration and potential edge cases may affect the estimate. title: Support for parsing maps

title: Several changes needed to Appc Private / Arrow Cloud install docs
description: See the details in the original ticket: https://techweb.axway.com/jira/browse/RDPP-595    * Overall: Is there any way to make the index topics links?  * -Page 11: Remove references to the Enterprise MBaaS. We can call it Appcelerator Private or Arrow, but the MBaaS and Enterprise MBaaS are references to ArrowDB which is just one component of the solution. Note that we will change the names once we agree upon new naming-  * -Page 12: Requirements. One of the first things on this page needs to be # of physical or virtual machines needed. For example, how many nodes are recommended in development/non-prod environments vs prod? Also which nodes should one place which services on?-  * -Page 13: SHouldn’t we reference the need for internet connectivity? Or in the case of absence thereof, the need for an internal npm registry mirror?-  * -Page 13: Has some TBD sections that don’t look good. We should fill them out or take them out.-  * Page 16,17,18 etc - -We reference ArrowCloud / ArrowDB all-in-one and App container base nodes. Can we include information someplace about what services are run on each of these nodes? This overview and terminology may be a good way to kick off the Pre-install section for the benefit of someone looking at this for the first time.-  * Pages 19, 20 -etc show pictures of ArrowCloud and ArrowDB architecture of processes for the first time.- We are breaking away from talking nodes/VMs on previous pages to talking processes for the first time, and it is not evident how these processes are spread out across nodes. Any information we can share here will help with adoption.  * -Page 21 talks about Developer Cloud on boarding - is there a reason we’re calling it that vs Developer on boarding?-  * -Page 21 references Appcelerator Studio. We support the use of Appcelerator Studio and the CLI both equally, in fact we’re steering away Arrow Development from Appcelerator Studio to a BYO-editor model. We should reference the CLI scenario here, and in fact take out references to Appc Studio.-  * -Page 47 talks about the SSL_PEM file. Note that PEM or Analytics is an optionally buyable separate product from Appcelerator Private / Appcelerator Arrow. In our documentation, we should first reference why you would want to use SSL_PEM (if you want to emit Analytics events to an external dashboard). Note also that starting with 1.5.4, you always use an on-premises dashboard when using Arrow Cloud behind the firewall. We no longer recommend the use of Arrow Cloud on-premises mixed in with a Cloud dashboard.-  * -Page 51 talks about deploying Arrow Cloud to VPC. This does not belong in Appcelerator Private or Appcelerator Arrow documentation.-  * -Page 56 talks about installing the Registry and Security server. We should first talk about installing the “dashboard”, which I don’t see referenced anywhere in the documentation.-  * -Page 61 talks about installing Registry server with Docker. Isn’t this a 1.6.0 thing?-  * -Page 65 talks about Developer Cloud Onboarding. See reference to Developer Cloud above.-

title: Extend the core Replication Framework to support operations on behalf of the Ingest system
description: The main objective of the development is to make extensions to the existing Replication System's framework to support the new Ingest system. Specifically, the following changes have been made in this context:   * extend the *Protobuf*-based protocol to support operations with the Qserv worker databases (inspecting, creating databases and tables, granting/revoking access privileges, operations with MySQL partitions, etc.)   * changes in the database schema and the *DatabaseServices* class to support super-transactions and chunk replicas in the """"being ingested"""" state   * implement a group of SQL requests and the worker-side support for them as per the above mentioned protocol extensions   * refactor and extend class *Controller* to support new types of requests   * extend the *ControllerApp* to allow testing new requests and the updated API of the *Controller*
